name: Aortic Regurgitation
signs:
Diastolic decrescendo murmur at left sternal border
Bounding (water-hammer) pulses
Widened pulse pressure
De Musset sign (head bobbing)
Muller sign (uvula pulsation)
Quincke sign (nail bed pulsations)
Dyspnea on exertion
Angina
treatment:
1. Medical management if asymptomatic with preserved EF
2. Surgery (AVR) when symptomatic or EF declining
3. Vasodilator therapy (limited role)
4. Emergent surgery for acute severe AR
5. Serial echo surveillance
meds:
Nifedipine 30-60mg daily (vasodilator, debated benefit)
ACE inhibitors for concurrent hypertension
Diuretics for volume overload
Surgery indications: symptoms, EF <55%, LVESD >50mm or LVEDD >65mm
ddx: Aortic stenosis, Mitral regurgitation, Pulmonary regurgitation, Patent ductus arteriosus, Austin Flint murmur (mimics mitral stenosis)
labs:
TTE with Doppler (regurgitant volume, jet width, PHT, LV dimensions)
TEE if better visualization needed
ECG (LVH)
CXR (cardiomegaly, dilated aorta)
CT/MRI aorta if root pathology
BNP
notes:
Acute AR (endocarditis, dissection, trauma): medical emergency, emergent surgery
Chronic AR: compensated for years, decompensation = irreversible LV damage
Eponymous signs reflect hyperdynamic circulation from wide pulse pressure
Austin Flint murmur: low-pitched diastolic rumble mimicking mitral stenosis
Causes: bicuspid aortic valve, rheumatic disease, endocarditis, aortic root dilation, Marfan
Surgery: valve repair emerging, but replacement still standard
description: Leaking of the aortic valve allowing blood to flow backward into the heart during relaxation, causing the heart to work harder and eventually enlarge.

name: Tricuspid Regurgitation
signs:
Holosystolic murmur at LLSB (increases with inspiration)
JVD with prominent V waves
Pulsatile liver (hepatomegaly)
Peripheral edema
Ascites
Fatigue
Right heart failure signs
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (left heart disease, pulmonary HTN)
2. Diuretics for volume overload
3. Surgical repair/replacement for severe symptomatic TR
4. Transcatheter tricuspid interventions (emerging)
5. Treat pulmonary hypertension if present
meds:
Furosemide 40-160mg daily
Spironolactone 25-50mg daily
Digoxin 0.125-0.25mg daily (rate control if AFib)
Pulmonary vasodilators if PH present
ddx: Mitral regurgitation, VSD, Pulmonary regurgitation, Carcinoid heart disease, Ebstein anomaly
labs:
TTE (TR jet velocity, RV size/function, RA size, IVC diameter)
ECG (RVH, AFib)
CXR (RA enlargement)
Right heart catheterization (hemodynamics)
CT/MRI (RV assessment)
Liver function tests
notes:
Functional TR (most common): from RV dilation secondary to left heart disease or PH
Primary TR: endocarditis (IVDU), carcinoid, Ebstein anomaly, rheumatic, pacemaker leads
Carvajal sign: increase in murmur with inspiration (right-sided murmurs)
Forgotten valve: often overlooked but impacts prognosis significantly
Tricuspid annuloplasty at time of left-sided valve surgery if significant TR
Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER): emerging option for high surgical risk
description: A condition where the tricuspid valve doesn't close properly, allowing blood to leak backward into the right atrium, often caused by enlargement of the right side of the heart.

name: Constrictive Pericarditis
signs:
JVD with Kussmaul sign (JVD increases with inspiration)
Peripheral edema and ascites
Pericardial knock (early diastolic sound)
Hepatomegaly
Fatigue and exercise intolerance
Pulsus paradoxus (uncommon)
Pericardial calcification on imaging
treatment:
1. Pericardiectomy (definitive treatment)
2. Diuretics (temporizing for volume overload)
3. Anti-inflammatory therapy for transient/early constrictive pericarditis
4. Treat underlying cause (TB, radiation, post-surgical)
meds:
Furosemide 40-80mg daily (temporizing)
Colchicine 0.5mg BID (early/transient constriction)
Ibuprofen 600mg TID (early/transient)
Preднисone if inflammatory etiology
Antitubercular therapy if TB-related
ddx: Restrictive cardiomyopathy, Cardiac tamponade, Right heart failure, Hepatic cirrhosis, Nephrotic syndrome, Budd-Chiari syndrome
labs:
Echo: septal bounce, respiratory variation in flow, annulus reversus (medial e' > lateral e')
CT chest (pericardial thickening >4mm, calcification)
Cardiac MRI (pericardial thickening, delayed enhancement)
Right heart catheterization (equalization of diastolic pressures, square root sign)
CBC, CRP, ESR
TB testing
BNP (typically normal or mildly elevated, helps distinguish from restrictive CM)
notes:
Causes: idiopathic/viral (most common in developed countries), TB (most common worldwide), post-surgical, radiation, connective tissue disease
Diastolic filling impaired by rigid pericardium → equalization of diastolic pressures
Constriction vs restriction: BNP lower in constriction, septal bounce on echo, MRI thickening
Transient constriction: 15-20% resolve with anti-inflammatory therapy without surgery
Pericardiectomy: complex surgery, 6-12% operative mortality
Kussmaul sign: distinguishes constriction (present) from tamponade (absent)
Occult constriction: unmasked by volume loading during catheterization
description: A condition where the pericardium becomes thick and stiff, constricting the heart and preventing it from filling properly, often requiring surgical removal of the pericardium.

name: Endometriosis
signs:
Cyclic pelvic pain (worse during menses)
Dysmenorrhea (severe)
Dyspareunia (deep)
Dyschezia (painful bowel movements during menses)
Infertility
Chronic pelvic pain
Adnexal mass (endometrioma)
Uterosacral nodularity on exam
Bloating
treatment:
1. NSAIDs for pain
2. Hormonal suppression: OCPs, progestins, GnRH agonists
3. Laparoscopic excision/ablation of lesions
4. Endometrioma excision (>4cm)
5. Fertility treatment if trying to conceive
6. Hysterectomy with BSO for definitive treatment (completed childbearing)
7. Multidisciplinary pain management
meds:
NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600mg TID, naproxen 500mg BID)
Combined OCPs (continuous, skip placebo)
Norethindrone acetate 5mg daily
Medroxyprogesterone 150mg IM q3 months (Depo-Provera)
Leuprolide 3.75mg IM monthly + norethindrone add-back (GnRH agonist)
Elagolix 150mg daily or 200mg BID (GnRH antagonist)
Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena)
ddx: PID, Ovarian cyst, Adenomyosis, Uterine fibroids, IBS, Interstitial cystitis, Chronic pelvic pain (other), Pelvic adhesions
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (history + exam)
Transvaginal ultrasound (endometriomas)
MRI pelvis (deep infiltrating endometriosis)
CA-125 (may be elevated, not diagnostic)
Laparoscopy with biopsy (gold standard for diagnosis)
Fertility workup if infertility
notes:
Affects 10% of reproductive-age women, 25-50% of infertile women
Gold standard diagnosis: laparoscopy with histologic confirmation
Powder burn lesions: classic appearance, but clear/red lesions may be more active
Endometrioma (chocolate cyst): ovarian endometriosis, old blood
Deep infiltrating endometriosis: rectovaginal, bladder, ureters
Staging (rASRM): I-minimal, II-mild, III-moderate, IV-severe (poor correlation with symptoms)
Retrograde menstruation theory most widely accepted
Adenomyosis: endometrial tissue within myometrium (distinct entity, similar symptoms)
description: A chronic condition where tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, causing pain, heavy periods, and infertility, affecting approximately one in ten women of reproductive age.

name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
signs:
Lower abdominal pain (bilateral)
Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign)
Adnexal tenderness
Abnormal vaginal or cervical discharge
Fever
Abnormal uterine bleeding
Dyspareunia
Right upper quadrant pain (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis)
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics covering N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis, and anaerobes
2. Outpatient treatment for mild-moderate
3. Hospitalize if: tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, severe illness, failed outpatient therapy
4. IUD can remain in place during treatment
5. Partner treatment (within 60 days)
6. STI screening and counseling
meds:
Outpatient: ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 + doxycycline 100mg BID x14 days + metronidazole 500mg BID x14 days
Inpatient: cefoxitin 2g IV q6h + doxycycline 100mg IV/PO BID
Inpatient alternative: clindamycin 900mg IV q8h + gentamicin 2mg/kg then 1.5mg/kg q8h
Transition to oral doxycycline + metronidazole after clinical improvement
ddx: Ectopic pregnancy, Appendicitis, Ovarian cyst/torsion, Endometriosis, UTI, Diverticulitis, IBD
labs:
Pregnancy test (rule out ectopic)
GC/chlamydia NAAT
CBC (leukocytosis)
CRP/ESR
Urinalysis
Wet prep (WBC predominance, clue cells)
Transvaginal ultrasound (TOA, free fluid)
CT if unclear diagnosis or TOA suspected
Blood cultures if septic
notes:
CDC diagnostic criteria: cervical motion tenderness OR uterine tenderness OR adnexal tenderness (low threshold for treatment)
GC and chlamydia most common, but often polymicrobial (BV organisms, anaerobes)
TOA: 15% of PID cases, may require drainage if >6cm or failing antibiotics
Fitz-Hugh-Curtis: perihepatitis, RUQ pain, violin-string adhesions on laparoscopy
Sequelae: infertility (20% after one episode), ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain
Risk increases with multiple sexual partners, history of STIs, douching
IUD: NOT a contraindication to treatment, does not need to be removed in most cases
description: An infection of the female reproductive organs usually caused by sexually transmitted bacteria, causing lower abdominal pain and risking infertility if not treated promptly with antibiotics.

name: Postpartum Hemorrhage
signs:
Excessive bleeding after delivery (>500mL vaginal, >1000mL cesarean)
Boggy uterus (atony)
Tachycardia
Hypotension
Pallor and diaphoresis
Large blood clots
Continuous slow bleeding (lacerations/retained tissue)
Coagulopathy signs
treatment:
1. Uterine massage (bimanual)
2. Uterotonic agents (oxytocin, methylergonovine, misoprostol)
3. IV fluids and blood products
4. Identify cause: 4 T's (Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin)
5. Uterine tamponade (balloon, packing)
6. Tranexamic acid within 3h
7. Surgical: B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy (last resort)
meds:
Oxytocin 10-40 units in 1L LR (first-line)
Methylergonovine 0.2mg IM q2-4h (avoid in HTN)
Carboprost (Hemabate) 250mcg IM q15min (max 8 doses, avoid in asthma)
Misoprostol 800-1000mcg rectal
Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10min (WOMAN trial)
PRBCs, FFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate (massive transfusion)
Calcium gluconate 1g IV (with massive transfusion)
ddx: Uterine atony (most common, 70-80%), Genital tract lacerations, Retained placenta/tissue, Coagulopathy (DIC), Uterine inversion, Placenta accreta spectrum
labs:
CBC (serial hemoglobin)
Coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen)
Type and crossmatch (order early)
BMP
Lactate
Thromboelastography (TEG) if available
Fibrinogen level (critical - <200mg/dL predicts severe PPH)
Kleihauer-Betke (if Rh-negative)
notes:
4 T's of PPH: Tone (70-80%), Trauma (lacerations, hematomas), Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy)
Risk factors: overdistended uterus (multiples, polyhydramnios, macrosomia), prolonged labor, chorioamnionitis, prior PPH
Fibrinogen <200mg/dL: 100% positive predictive value for severe PPH
Tranexamic acid: give within 3h of delivery (WOMAN trial showed reduced death from bleeding)
Placenta accreta spectrum: abnormal placental attachment, major cause of hemorrhage requiring hysterectomy
Massive transfusion protocol: 1:1:1 ratio of RBC:FFP:platelets
QBL (quantitative blood loss): better than visual estimation for identifying PPH
description: Excessive bleeding after childbirth, most commonly caused by the uterus not contracting properly, requiring rapid treatment with medications, procedures, and potentially surgery to prevent maternal death.

name: Gestational Diabetes
signs:
Often asymptomatic (detected by screening)
Polyuria and polydipsia (if severe)
Recurrent vaginal candidiasis
Macrosomia (large-for-gestational-age fetus)
Polyhydramnios
Excessive weight gain
treatment:
1. Medical nutrition therapy and carbohydrate counting
2. Self-monitoring of blood glucose (fasting + 1-2h postprandial)
3. Exercise (30 min moderate activity most days)
4. Insulin if diet/exercise insufficient
5. Metformin or glyburide (alternatives to insulin)
6. Fetal surveillance (NST, growth ultrasounds)
7. Delivery planning (39 weeks if insulin-requiring)
8. Postpartum glucose screening at 4-12 weeks
meds:
Insulin: NPH + regular/lispro/aspart (preferred pharmacotherapy)
Starting insulin dose: 0.7-1.0 units/kg/day (increases through pregnancy)
Metformin 500mg BID titrate to 2500mg (crosses placenta)
Glyburide 2.5-20mg daily (less recommended, crosses placenta)
Targets: fasting <95, 1h post-meal <140, 2h post-meal <120
ddx: Pre-existing type 1 or type 2 diabetes, Stress hyperglycemia, Cushing syndrome, Medication-induced (steroids for fetal lung maturity)
labs:
Screening at 24-28 weeks: 50g GCT (1h glucose >=130-140 → diagnostic test)
Diagnostic: 100g OGTT (two or more abnormal: fasting >=95, 1h >=180, 2h >=155, 3h >=140)
Alternative: 75g OGTT (one abnormal value diagnostic: fasting >=92, 1h >=180, 2h >=153)
HbA1c (limited value due to physiologic changes in pregnancy)
Fasting glucose at first prenatal visit (screen for pre-existing DM)
Postpartum: 75g OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum
notes:
Affects 6-9% of pregnancies
Risk factors: obesity, age >25, family history DM, previous GDM, previous macrosomia, PCOS
Complications: macrosomia, birth trauma (shoulder dystocia), neonatal hypoglycemia, preeclampsia, cesarean delivery
Fetal risks: macrosomia, hypoglycemia, hyperbilirubinemia, polycythemia, respiratory distress
GDM increases lifetime risk of type 2 DM: 50% within 5-10 years
Early screening (<20 weeks) for high-risk women
Metformin: increasingly used but crosses placenta (long-term pediatric effects being studied)
Insulin does NOT cross placenta - safest pharmacotherapy
description: Diabetes first diagnosed during pregnancy that increases risks to both mother and baby, managed with diet, exercise, and often insulin to maintain healthy blood sugar levels.

name: HELLP Syndrome
signs:
RUQ or epigastric pain
Nausea and vomiting
Malaise
Hypertension (may be absent in 15%)
Edema
Jaundice
Proteinuria
Bleeding (DIC)
Visual changes
treatment:
1. Delivery is definitive treatment
2. If >=34 weeks: deliver
3. If <34 weeks: corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity then deliver within 24-48h
4. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
5. Blood pressure control
6. Platelet and blood product transfusion as needed
7. Monitoring for DIC, hepatic rupture, renal failure
meds:
Betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart (fetal lungs, <34 weeks)
Magnesium sulfate 4-6g IV bolus then 1-2g/hr
Labetalol or nifedipine for BP control
Platelet transfusion if <20K or <50K with active bleeding/surgery
Dexamethasone 10mg IV q12h (may improve platelet count, controversial for maternal benefit)
PRBCs, FFP, cryoprecipitate as needed
ddx: Preeclampsia without HELLP, Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, TTP/HUS, Viral hepatitis, Cholecystitis, Pancreatitis, SLE flare, Antiphospholipid syndrome
labs:
CBC: platelets <100,000 (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets)
Peripheral smear: schistocytes, helmet cells (microangiopathic hemolysis)
AST/ALT elevated (>2x normal)
LDH elevated (>600 IU/L)
Total bilirubin elevated (>1.2)
Haptoglobin decreased
Coagulation studies (DIC screening)
Fibrinogen
BMP (creatinine)
Urine protein
notes:
Occurs in 10-20% of severe preeclampsia patients
Can occur without significant hypertension or proteinuria (diagnostic pitfall)
Mississippi classification: Class 1 (platelets <50K, worst), Class 2 (50-100K), Class 3 (100-150K)
Complications: DIC (20%), placental abruption (16%), AKI, hepatic rupture/infarction, ARDS, stroke
Hepatic rupture: rare but catastrophic - RUQ pain, shoulder pain, shock
Usually resolves within 48-72h postpartum (platelets nadir at 24-48h then recover)
Recurrence risk: 3-25% in subsequent pregnancies
May occur up to 7 days postpartum
description: A serious pregnancy complication involving breakdown of red blood cells, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count, usually occurring in the context of preeclampsia and requiring urgent delivery.

name: Open-Angle Glaucoma
signs:
Gradual painless vision loss
Peripheral visual field loss first (tunnel vision late)
Elevated intraocular pressure (>21mmHg, but may be normal)
Optic disc cupping (increased cup-to-disc ratio >0.5)
Asymmetric cupping between eyes
Often asymptomatic until advanced
Nerve fiber layer thinning on OCT
treatment:
1. Topical IOP-lowering drops (first-line)
2. Target IOP reduction of 20-30% from baseline
3. Laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) - can be first-line
4. Trabeculectomy or tube shunt for refractory
5. MIGS (minimally invasive glaucoma surgery)
6. Regular monitoring: IOP, visual fields, OCT
meds:
Latanoprost 0.005% 1 drop QHS (prostaglandin analog, first-line)
Timolol 0.5% 1 drop BID (beta-blocker)
Brimonidine 0.2% 1 drop BID-TID (alpha-agonist)
Dorzolamide 2% 1 drop TID (CAI)
Fixed combinations: dorzolamide/timolol BID
Netarsudil 0.02% 1 drop QHS (Rho kinase inhibitor)
Latanoprostene bunod 0.024% QHS
ddx: Normal tension glaucoma, Ocular hypertension (no optic nerve damage), Angle-closure glaucoma, Secondary glaucoma, Optic neuritis, Compressive optic neuropathy
labs:
Tonometry (IOP measurement)
Gonioscopy (open angle confirmation)
OCT (RNFL and ganglion cell analysis)
Visual field testing (Humphrey perimetry)
Fundoscopy (cup-to-disc ratio, disc hemorrhages)
Pachymetry (corneal thickness affects IOP reading)
Diurnal IOP curves if suspected fluctuation
notes:
Leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide
Risk factors: age >40, African American descent, family history, elevated IOP, myopia, thin central cornea
Normal-tension glaucoma: optic nerve damage with IOP <21 (common in Asian populations)
Screening: dilated eye exam every 1-2 years after age 40 (more frequent if high risk)
SLT (selective laser trabeculoplasty): can be first-line, repeatable, 70-80% success
Prostaglandin analogs: may cause iris color change, eyelash growth, periorbital fat loss
Adherence: major challenge - simplify regimen, educate patient
Vision loss is irreversible - goal is to preserve remaining vision
description: A chronic eye disease where gradual damage to the optic nerve causes progressive peripheral vision loss, usually associated with elevated eye pressure, often without symptoms until advanced.

name: Diabetic Retinopathy
signs:
Often asymptomatic in early stages
Blurred vision
Floaters
Fluctuating vision
Microaneurysms (earliest sign)
Hard exudates
Cotton-wool spots
Intraretinal hemorrhages
Neovascularization (proliferative)
Vitreous hemorrhage
Tractional retinal detachment (advanced)
treatment:
1. Tight glycemic control (HbA1c <7%)
2. Blood pressure control
3. Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (mainstay for DME and PDR)
4. Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) for PDR
5. Focal/grid laser for clinically significant DME (less used now)
6. Vitrectomy for non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage or tractional RD
7. Annual dilated eye exams for all diabetics
meds:
Ranibizumab 0.3mg intravitreal injection monthly (DME)
Aflibercept 2mg intravitreal q4-8 weeks (DME/PDR)
Bevacizumab 1.25mg intravitreal (off-label, cost-effective)
Faricimab intravitreal q4-16 weeks (anti-VEGF + anti-Ang2)
Intravitreal dexamethasone implant 0.7mg (DME, refractory)
Fenofibrate 145-160mg daily (may reduce progression)
Optimize: insulin, antihypertensives, statins
ddx: Hypertensive retinopathy, Retinal vein occlusion, Radiation retinopathy, Ocular ischemic syndrome, Sickle cell retinopathy, Eales disease
labs:
Dilated fundus exam (gold standard for screening)
Fluorescein angiography (leakage, ischemia, neovascularization)
OCT (macular edema thickness measurement)
OCT angiography (noninvasive vascular imaging)
HbA1c, blood glucose
Blood pressure
Lipid panel
BMP (renal function - often concurrent diabetic nephropathy)
notes:
Screening: annual dilated exam starting at diagnosis (type 2) or 5 years after diagnosis (type 1)
DR staging: mild/moderate/severe NPDR → PDR
DME can occur at any stage (leading cause of vision loss in diabetic patients)
Anti-VEGF: preferred over laser for center-involving DME (DRCR.net Protocol T)
PRP: still used for high-risk PDR, but anti-VEGF increasingly used (Protocol S)
Pregnancy: DR can worsen rapidly - screen each trimester
Every 1% reduction in HbA1c reduces DR risk by 40%
Diabetic kidney disease and retinopathy often progress together
description: Damage to the retinal blood vessels caused by chronic diabetes, progressing from mild changes to vision-threatening complications like macular swelling and abnormal blood vessel growth.

name: Uveitis
signs:
Eye pain
Photophobia
Blurred vision
Tearing
Red eye (ciliary flush)
Floaters (posterior uveitis)
Miosis (constricted pupil)
Cells and flare in anterior chamber
Hypopyon (severe anterior uveitis)
Synechiae (iris adhesions)
treatment:
1. Topical corticosteroid drops (anterior uveitis)
2. Cycloplegic drops (pain relief, prevent synechiae)
3. Systemic immunosuppression for chronic/posterior/panuveitis
4. Intravitreal injections for intermediate/posterior
5. Identify and treat underlying systemic disease
6. Ophthalmology referral for all uveitis
meds:
Prednisolone acetate 1% 1 drop q1-2h then taper
Cyclopentolate 1% BID-TID (cycloplegic)
Atropine 1% BID (severe cases)
Prednisone 1mg/kg PO (systemic, non-infectious posterior)
Methotrexate 15-25mg weekly (steroid-sparing)
Adalimumab 40mg SC q2wk (non-infectious intermediate/posterior/panuveitis)
Intravitreal dexamethasone implant
Antimicrobials if infectious etiology
ddx: Conjunctivitis, Acute angle-closure glaucoma, Scleritis/episcleritis, Endophthalmitis, Corneal ulcer, Keratitis, Retinal detachment
labs:
Slit lamp examination (cells, flare, keratic precipitates)
Fundoscopy (vitritis, retinal lesions)
HLA-B27 (ankylosing spondylitis association)
ACE level, chest XR (sarcoidosis)
RPR/FTA-ABS (syphilis)
QuantiFERON/PPD (TB)
ANA (juvenile idiopathic arthritis)
Lyme serology (endemic areas)
Toxoplasma IgG
OCT (macular edema)
Fluorescein angiography
notes:
Classification by location: anterior (most common), intermediate, posterior, panuveitis
HLA-B27 anterior uveitis: young males, unilateral, acute, recurrent, associated with ankylosing spondylitis
Sarcoidosis: granulomatous uveitis with mutton-fat KPs and tent-shaped synechiae
Toxoplasmosis: most common cause of posterior uveitis worldwide
Behcet disease: panuveitis with hypopyon, oral/genital ulcers
JIA: chronic anterior uveitis in children (asymptomatic white eye - screen regularly)
Infectious must be ruled out before immunosuppression
Complications: cataracts, glaucoma, macular edema, band keratopathy
description: Inflammation of the middle layer of the eye (uvea), causing eye pain, redness, and blurred vision, often associated with autoimmune conditions and requiring prompt treatment to prevent vision loss.

name: Epiglottitis
signs:
Acute onset sore throat and odynophagia
Muffled (hot potato) voice
Drooling
Tripod position (sitting upright, leaning forward)
Stridor (late, ominous)
High fever
Anxious appearance
Respiratory distress
Swollen, cherry-red epiglottis (do NOT examine unless airway secured in children)
treatment:
1. Airway management is TOP PRIORITY
2. Keep patient calm, do NOT agitate
3. Controlled intubation in OR (preferred) with ENT/anesthesia present
4. IV antibiotics
5. IV dexamethasone (reduce edema)
6. Humidified oxygen
7. ICU admission for airway monitoring
meds:
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily or cefotaxime 2g IV q8h
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg IV (max 10mg)
Epinephrine racemic nebulized (temporizing for stridor)
Vancomycin if MRSA concern
Hib vaccine (prevention in children)
ddx: Croup, Peritonsillar abscess, Retropharyngeal abscess, Foreign body aspiration, Angioedema, Bacterial tracheitis, Diphtheria, Ludwig's angina
labs:
Lateral neck XR: thumbprint sign (swollen epiglottis) - only if stable, do NOT delay airway management
CT neck (if diagnosis uncertain and airway stable)
Blood cultures
CBC (leukocytosis)
Epiglottic culture (after airway secured)
Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (adults, carefully in children)
notes:
Historically H. influenzae type b in children (now rare due to Hib vaccine)
Adults: now more common than children, often S. aureus, Strep species, H. influenzae (non-typeable)
Do NOT examine pharynx in children with suspected epiglottitis (can cause laryngospasm and complete obstruction)
XR thumb sign: swollen epiglottis, loss of vallecula (only obtain if airway is stable)
Adults: less likely to have sudden complete obstruction, but still possible
Mortality: <1% with appropriate management, higher if airway lost
Hib vaccination has dramatically reduced pediatric epiglottitis
description: A potentially life-threatening inflammation of the epiglottis that can rapidly block the airway, requiring urgent airway management and IV antibiotics.

name: Meniere's Disease
signs:
Episodic vertigo (20 min to 12h)
Fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss (low frequency initially)
Tinnitus (roaring or buzzing)
Aural fullness (ear pressure)
Nausea and vomiting during attacks
Nystagmus during acute episodes
Hearing loss becomes permanent over time
treatment:
1. Low-sodium diet (<1500mg/day)
2. Diuretics (thiazide or acetazolamide)
3. Vestibular suppressants during acute attacks
4. Betahistine (used outside US)
5. Intratympanic dexamethasone for refractory cases
6. Intratympanic gentamicin (chemical labyrinthectomy)
7. Endolymphatic sac surgery or vestibular nerve section (last resort)
meds:
Meclizine 25mg q6h PRN (acute attack)
Dimenhydrinate 50mg q6h PRN (acute)
Ondansetron 4-8mg (nausea)
Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily + triamterene (diuretic)
Betahistine 16mg TID (not available in US)
Intratympanic dexamethasone 4mg/mL (refractory)
Intratympanic gentamicin (vestibulotoxic, controls vertigo)
Diazepam 5mg PRN (severe acute attacks)
ddx: BPPV, Vestibular migraine, Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma), Labyrinthitis, Perilymphatic fistula, Superior semicircular canal dehiscence, Otosyphilis, Autoimmune inner ear disease
labs:
Audiometry (low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss)
VidENG/ENG (caloric testing may show unilateral weakness)
Electrocochleography (elevated SP/AP ratio)
MRI with gadolinium (rule out vestibular schwannoma)
ABR (auditory brainstem response)
VEMP (vestibular evoked myogenic potentials)
notes:
Endolymphatic hydrops: distension of endolymphatic space is pathologic hallmark
AAO-HNS criteria: >=2 episodes of vertigo (20 min-12h), audiometrically documented SNHL, tinnitus or aural fullness
Natural history: vertigo attacks may decrease over years but hearing loss progresses
Bilateral in 25-50% over time
Tumarkin crises (drop attacks): sudden falls without LOC, due to otolith dysfunction
Sodium restriction + diuretics: first-line, helps 60-80%
Intratympanic gentamicin: controls vertigo in >90% but risks further hearing loss
Vestibular schwannoma must be excluded with MRI (unilateral symptoms)
description: A chronic inner ear disorder causing episodes of spinning vertigo, hearing loss, ringing in the ear, and a feeling of ear fullness, caused by abnormal fluid buildup in the inner ear.

name: Sinusitis
signs:
Facial pain/pressure (worse leaning forward)
Nasal congestion and obstruction
Purulent nasal discharge
Hyposmia or anosmia
Postnasal drip
Cough (worse at night)
Fever
Dental pain (maxillary sinusitis)
Periorbital swelling (complication)
treatment:
1. Acute viral (<10 days): supportive care only
2. Acute bacterial (>10 days or biphasic): antibiotics
3. Saline nasal irrigation (evidence-based, all types)
4. Intranasal corticosteroids
5. Decongestants short-term
6. Surgery (FESS) for chronic refractory sinusitis
7. Avoid antibiotics for viral sinusitis
meds:
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg BID x5-7 days (bacterial, first-line)
Amoxicillin 1g TID (alternative first-line)
Doxycycline 100mg BID x5-7 days (penicillin allergy)
Fluticasone propionate nasal spray 2 sprays BID
Saline nasal irrigation (neti pot, sinus rinse)
Oxymetazoline nasal spray (max 3 days)
Pseudoephedrine 60mg q6h (systemic decongestant)
Guaifenesin 600mg BID (mucolytic)
ddx: Allergic rhinitis, Migraine/tension headache, Dental infection, Nasal polyps, Fungal sinusitis, Wegener granulomatosis, Nasopharyngeal carcinoma, CSF rhinorrhea
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (imaging not needed for uncomplicated acute)
CT sinuses (chronic sinusitis, surgical planning, complications)
Nasal endoscopy (chronic, recurrent)
Culture (endoscopic-guided, chronic/refractory)
CBC if complication suspected
CT with contrast if orbital or intracranial complication suspected
notes:
Viral: 90-98% of acute sinusitis, resolves in 7-10 days
Bacterial: suspect if symptoms >10 days, worsening after initial improvement (double-sickening), or severe onset (>=3 days high fever + purulent discharge)
Most common bacteria: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis
Complications: orbital cellulitis/abscess, intracranial abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis
Pott's puffy tumor: subperiosteal frontal bone abscess (frontal sinusitis complication)
Chronic sinusitis: >12 weeks, may need CT, endoscopy, and surgery (FESS)
Allergic fungal sinusitis: thick eoite mucin with fungal elements, treat surgically + steroids
Samter triad: nasal polyps + asthma + aspirin sensitivity
description: Inflammation of the sinuses usually caused by viral infection, with bacterial sinusitis suspected when symptoms persist beyond 10 days or worsen after initial improvement.

name: Otitis Media
signs:
Ear pain (otalgia)
Bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane
Decreased TM mobility (pneumatic otoscopy)
Conductive hearing loss
Fever
Irritability (infants)
Otorrhea (if TM perforated)
Effusion behind TM
treatment:
1. Pain management (first priority)
2. Observation for mild cases in children >=2 years (watchful waiting 48-72h)
3. Antibiotics if <2 years, bilateral, severe symptoms, otorrhea
4. High-dose amoxicillin first-line
5. Tympanostomy tubes for recurrent AOM (>=3 in 6 months or >=4 in 12 months)
6. Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination (prevention)
meds:
Amoxicillin 80-90mg/kg/day divided BID x10 days (first-line, children)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 90/6.4mg/kg/day BID (treatment failure)
Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM x1-3 days (vomiting or failed oral)
Ibuprofen 10mg/kg q6h (pain)
Acetaminophen 15mg/kg q4h (pain)
Topical benzocaine drops (temporary pain relief)
Amoxicillin 500mg TID x5-7 days (adults)
ddx: Otitis externa, Referred pain (dental, TMJ, pharyngitis), Mastoiditis, Cholesteatoma, Foreign body, Myringitis, Ramsay Hunt syndrome
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (otoscopy)
Pneumatic otoscopy (decreased TM mobility, most specific)
Tympanometry (type B = effusion)
No imaging needed for uncomplicated AOM
CT temporal bone if mastoiditis suspected
Tympanocentesis culture (refractory cases)
notes:
Most common childhood infection requiring antibiotics
Peak age: 6-18 months
Most common organisms: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae (non-typeable), M. catarrhalis
Watchful waiting criteria (no antibiotics): age >=2, unilateral, non-severe (mild pain <48h, temp <102.2°F)
Recurrent AOM: >=3 episodes in 6 months or >=4 in 12 months → tympanostomy tubes
OME (otitis media with effusion): watchful waiting 3 months, no antibiotics (sterile fluid)
Complications: mastoiditis, hearing loss, TM perforation, cholesteatoma, intracranial infection
Breastfeeding, avoiding smoke exposure, and vaccination reduce AOM risk
description: A middle ear infection most common in young children, causing ear pain and a bulging eardrum, treated with pain management and antibiotics when indicated.

name: Epistaxis
signs:
Active nasal bleeding
Anterior bleeding (90%): from Kiesselbach plexus (Little's area)
Posterior bleeding (10%): profuse, from sphenopalatine artery, drains into pharynx
Blood-tinged saliva
Hematemesis (swallowed blood)
Hemodynamic instability (severe posterior)
treatment:
1. Direct pressure: pinch nostrils firmly x15-20 minutes (lean forward)
2. Topical vasoconstrictors (oxymetazoline)
3. Anterior packing (if pressure fails)
4. Chemical cautery (silver nitrate) for visible anterior bleeding point
5. Posterior packing (Foley balloon, posterior nasal pack) for posterior bleeds
6. Sphenopalatine artery ligation or embolization for refractory posterior
7. Correct coagulopathy and anticoagulation if applicable
meds:
Oxymetazoline 0.05% spray (topical vasoconstrictor)
Silver nitrate cautery sticks (chemical cautery)
Tranexamic acid soaked cotton pledget (topical)
Topical thrombin
Surgicel or gelatin sponge (hemostatic agents)
Antibiotic ointment (bacitracin, mupirocin) to prevent crusting
Saline nasal spray (humidification, prevention)
ddx: Nasal trauma, Nasal foreign body, Coagulopathy (warfarin, DOACs), Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, Nasal tumor, Hypertension (association, not direct cause), Leukemia, Thrombocytopenia
labs:
CBC (hemoglobin, platelet count)
Coagulation studies (PT/INR, PTT) if anticoagulated or recurrent
Type and crossmatch (if significant bleeding)
BMP
Nasal endoscopy (identify bleeding source, rule out tumor)
Angiography if embolization planned
notes:
Anterior (90%): Kiesselbach plexus (Little's area) on anterior septum, usually self-limited
Posterior (10%): sphenopalatine artery, more difficult to control, more common in elderly/anticoagulated
Initial management: sit upright, lean forward, pinch soft part of nose x15-20 minutes continuously
Anterior packing: Rapid Rhino or Merocel sponge, leave 24-48h, cover with antibiotics (TSS risk)
Posterior packing: admission required (airway compromise risk, vagal response)
HHT (Osler-Weber-Rendu): recurrent severe epistaxis, telangiectasias, family history, AVM screening
Anticoagulation: balance bleeding risk vs thrombotic risk, don't reflexively reverse unless life-threatening
Prevention: humidification, saline spray, petroleum jelly, avoid digital trauma
description: Nosebleeds, usually from blood vessels in the front of the nasal septum, ranging from minor self-limited episodes to severe posterior bleeding requiring emergency intervention.
